1: Ps 107: The Great Thanksgiving Psalm — Four Rescues, One Refrain. Ps 107 opens Book V of the Psalter with a joyful, liturgical thanksgiving that moves through four vignettes of divine rescue. Each section follows the same narrative arc: danger → cry to YHWH → rescue → thanksgiving refrain. The four groups (wilderness wanderers, vv4-9; prisoners, vv10-16; the sick, vv17-22; sailors in a storm, vv23-32) may represent types of exilic and post-exilic experience. The refrain 'O give thanks to the LORD, for he is good, for his steadfast love endures forever' (ki tov ki leolam chasdo) is the great liturgical formula of Israel's worship (1 Chronicles 16:34; Psalms 106:1; Psalms 118:1; 136 passim). The formula is not merely doxological — it anchors the thanksgiving in the covenant term chesed: YHWH's loyal, covenant-obligated love is what makes every rescue possible.
2: 'Let the redeemed of the LORD say so, whom he has redeemed from trouble' — yomeru geulei YHWH (let the redeemed of the LORD say so). The 'redeemed' (geulim) are those who have been ga'al-ed — redeemed through the kinsman-redeemer mechanism (the go'el: the nearest relative who pays the price to buy back a family member from debt-slavery or captivity). YHWH as the divine go'el is a major theological motif of Isa 40-55 (Isaiah 41:14; Psalms 43:1; Psalms 44:22-24; Psalms 54:5). The 'trouble' (tzar, adversity/distress) from which they are redeemed echoes the Egyptian bondage. The command to 'say so' is a liturgical mandate: the rescued community is obligated to public testimony.
3: '...and gathered in from the lands, from the east and from the west, from the north and from the south' — the four-direction gathering is the post-exilic ingathering formula (Isaiah 43:5-6: 'I will bring your offspring from the east... from the west... from the north... from the south'). The LXX adds 'and from the sea' (cf. Psalms 107:23-32). This verse locates Ps 107 in the post-exilic context: the 'redeemed' are specifically those gathered from the Diaspora nations where they had been scattered by the Assyrian and Babylonian deportations.
4: 'Some wandered in desert wastes, finding no way to a city to dwell in' — the first vignette: wilderness wanderers. 'Desert wastes' (midbar yeshimon) — the same language as the Sinai wilderness (Deuteronomy 32:10: 'He found him in a desert land and in the howling waste of the wilderness'). The 'city to dwell in' (ir moshav) — the settled, inhabited place that the wanderer cannot find. This is the exile's experience: scattered from the homeland, wandering without a city of rest.
5: 'Hungry and thirsty, their soul fainted within them' — the physical extremity of the wilderness: reevim vegam tzemaim (hungry and also thirsty). The Exodus parallels: Exod 16 (hunger: the manna) and Exod 17 (thirst: Meribah water). The 'fainted soul' (naphsham titateeph) is the near-death exhaustion of prolonged deprivation. The soul (naphesh) — here the seat of bodily life and appetite — is failing.
6: 'Then they cried to the LORD in their trouble, and he delivered them from their distress' — the first occurrence of the rescue formula (vayitze'aqu el-YHWH batzar lahem mimetzukoteihem yatziltem). The cry (tza'aq) is the emergency summons — the same verb used for Israel's cry from Egyptian bondage (Exodus 2:23). The divine response: 'he delivered them' (yatziltem, Hiphil of natzal, to snatch from danger). The rescue is immediate — no delay once the cry goes up.
7: 'He led them by a straight way till they reached a city to dwell in' — the divine guidance: derekh yeshara (a straight/direct way) — in contrast to the wandering of vv4-5. The 'city to dwell in' — now they reach what they had failed to find. The Exodus pattern: YHWH led Israel through the wilderness by the pillar of cloud and fire (Exodus 13:21) on a path that the wanderers alone could not find. The second Exodus (return from exile) follows the same pattern (Isaiah 40:3; Psalms 43:19).
8: 'Let them thank the LORD for his steadfast love, for his wondrous works to the children of man!' — the first occurrence of the thanksgiving refrain (also at vv15, 21, 31). The congregation is summoned to thank (yodu, Hiphil of yadah, to give public acknowledgment/thanksgiving) YHWH for both his chesed (steadfast/covenant love) and his niphlaot (wondrous works). The two grounds of thanksgiving are YHWH's character (chesed) and YHWH's acts (niphlaot).
9: 'For he satisfies the longing soul, and the hungry soul he fills with good things' — the specific content of the rescue: provision for physical need. 'Longing soul' (naphesh shaqeqah) — the soul that thirsts intensely — satisfied (savet, given its fill). 'The hungry soul' (naphesh reevah) filled with 'good things' (tov). The satisfaction language echoes Psalms 103:5 ('who satisfies you with good so that your youth is renewed like the eagle's'). The physical provision is also spiritual fulfillment — the whole person is addressed.
10: 'Some sat in darkness and in the shadow of death, prisoners in affliction and in irons' — the second vignette: prisoners. 'Shadow of death' (tzalmavet) — the deep darkness of the underworld or of mortal danger; cf. Psalms 23:4. 'Affliction and in irons' (oni uvarzel) — captivity imagery: the iron fetters of the prisoner. In the ANE, prisoners were kept in underground dungeons and bound with bronze or iron chains. This vignette maps onto the Babylonian exile: the deportees imprisoned in a foreign land under foreign law.
11: 'For they had rebelled against the words of God, and spurned the counsel of the Most High' — the moral cause of their captivity: rebellion (maru, Piel of marah) against 'the words of God' (imrei El) and contempt for 'the counsel of the Most High' (atzat Elyon). The exile is not merely a geopolitical catastrophe but a covenantal consequence: the prophets had warned, the people had rejected the warning, and the Babylonian captivity was the curse of Deuteronomy 28:36-37 ('the LORD will bring you and your king to a nation neither you nor your fathers have known').
12: 'So he bowed their hearts down with hard labor; they fell down, with none to help' — the hardship of captivity: vayachna beamal libbam (he humbled their heart with labor). YHWH himself is the agent of the humiliation — the captivity is the divine rod of discipline. 'Fell down with none to help' (koshlu vein ozer) — the characteristic desolation of the abandoned: no human helper, the vacuum that makes the divine rescue uniquely meaningful.
13: 'Then they cried to the LORD in their trouble, and he delivered them from their distress' — the rescue formula (second occurrence). The pattern holds even for those whose captivity was their own fault (v11): the divine responsiveness to the cry (tza'aq) is not conditioned on the righteousness of the crier. This is a key theological point — YHWH hears even the disciplined rebel who returns to cry out.
14: 'He brought them out of darkness and the shadow of death, and burst their bonds apart' — the rescue from captivity: 'brought them out of darkness' (yotziim mechoshech) echoes the Exodus language (Exodus 20:2: 'I am the LORD who brought you out of Egypt'). 'Burst their bonds apart' (umosroteihem ynateq) — the severing of the iron fetters. The prison liberation anticipates the NT image of Christ as the one who sets captives free (Isaiah 61:1; Luke 4:18; John 8:36).
15: 'Let them thank the LORD for his steadfast love, for his wondrous works to the children of man!' — the refrain (second). The wondrous works (niphlaot) for the second group are liberation from imprisonment: the divine power to break physical chains. The 'children of man' (bene adam) includes all humanity — YHWH's rescue is not restricted to Israel but the psalm's primary reference is the covenant people.
16: 'For he shatters the doors of bronze and cuts in two the bars of iron' — 'doors of bronze' (daltot nechoshet) and 'bars of iron' (brichei barzel) — the strongest ANE prison materials. Isaiah 45:2 applies identical language to YHWH's action through Cyrus: 'I will break in pieces the doors of bronze and cut through the bars of iron.' The psalmist may have this Isaianic text in mind: the Persian liberation of the Jews from Babylonian captivity as YHWH shattering the prison doors.
17: 'Some were fools through their sinful ways, and because of their iniquities suffered affliction' — the third vignette: the sick. 'Fools' (evilim) — those who act without wisdom or covenant understanding, not merely intellectually deficient but morally foolish. Their sickness is directly tied to their 'sinful ways' (derekh pesha'am) and 'iniquities' (avonot): the OT connection between sin and sickness is a theological principle (Psalms 38:3-5; John 9:2-3 must be read against this background, where Jesus complicates the simple equation).
18: 'They loathed any kind of food, and they drew near to the gates of death' — the sickness's physical extremity: 'loathed any kind of food' (kol ochel tetalev naphsham) — the appetite for life itself is gone. 'Gates of death' (sha'are mavet) — the ANE imagery of death as a city with gates into Sheol; cf. Isaiah 38:10; Matthew 16:18. The sick person has approached to the threshold of the underworld.
19: 'Then they cried to the LORD in their trouble, and he delivered them from their distress' — the rescue formula (third occurrence). The same pattern: even those whose suffering arose from their own foolishness cry out and YHWH responds. The rescue formula's repetition without qualification shows that the divine rescuing does not require the petitioner to have been innocent — only that they cry to YHWH.
20: 'He sent out his word and healed them, and delivered them from their destruction' — 'He sent out his word' (yishlach devaro) — the divine word as healing agent. YHWH's healing comes through his spoken word: the same word by which he created (Gen 1) also heals and restores. The 'word' (dabar) that heals here becomes, in the NT, the Word who takes flesh (John 1:1-14) and heals by his presence. Matthew 8:8 (the centurion: 'only say the word') connects to this tradition.
21: 'Let them thank the LORD for his steadfast love, for his wondrous works to the children of man!' — the refrain (third). The wondrous work for this group is healing — the divine word's power over sickness. The refrain's repetition builds momentum toward the fourth and climactic vignette.
22: 'And let them offer sacrifices of thanksgiving, and tell of his deeds in songs of joy!' — the liturgical response to healing: 'sacrifices of thanksgiving' (zivchei todah) — the specific category of peace-offering made in fulfilment of a vow after deliverance from danger (Leviticus 7:12-15). The todah sacrifice required the whole community to eat together, accompanied by specific types of unleavened bread. The public 'songs of joy' (berinanah yesapperu) are the verbal testimony accompanying the sacrifice. Healing demands both liturgical and verbal response.
23: 'Some went down to the sea in ships, doing business on the great waters' — the fourth vignette: sailors. This is the only maritime passage in the Psalter (Pss 93 and 29 reference the sea symbolically; this is actual maritime commerce). The ancient Mediterranean trade routes: 'going down to the sea in ships' (yordei hayam be'oniyot) was the description of Phoenician and coastal merchants whose livelihoods depended on sea-voyages. The 'great waters' (mayim rabbim) — the cosmological term for the chaotic waters over which YHWH has sovereignty.
24: 'They saw the deeds of the LORD, his wondrous works in the deep' — the sailors have a unique vantage point: they see YHWH's wondrous works (niphlaot) specifically 'in the deep' (bametzulah). The deep (metzulah) is the furthest reach of the sea — the abyssal realm where YHWH's power is most dramatically visible to those who venture there.
25: 'For he commanded and raised the stormy wind, which lifted up the waves of the sea' — YHWH commands (yetzav) the storm wind (ruach searah): the storm is not natural disaster but divine action. The 'lifting up' (varomer galav) of the waves — the sea's waves under divine control. Ps 29 describes the same storm-YHWH theology. The ANE background: Baal was the storm-god in Ugaritic mythology; Israel's psalm claims the storm for YHWH.
26: 'They mounted up to heaven; they went down to the depths; their courage melted away in their evil plight' — the sailors' terrifying experience: 'mounted up to heaven' — the ship cresting a wave; 'went down to the depths' (yerdeu tehomot) — plunging into the trough. The alternation creates dread. 'Their courage melted away' (naphsham beraah titmogeg, their soul melted in their distress) — the visceral fear response.
27: 'They reeled and staggered like drunken men and were at their wits' end' — 'reeled and staggered like drunken men' (yachoggu veyanu'u kashikkor) — the rolling of the ship makes the sailors appear drunk: a striking maritime observation of the effects of rough seas. 'Wits' end' (kol chochmatam titballa, all their wisdom was swallowed up) — their navigational skill and seamanship, all their expertise, is consumed by the storm they cannot control.
28: 'Then they cried to the LORD in their trouble, and he delivered them from their distress' — the rescue formula (fourth and final occurrence). Even the sailors — not a group typically associated with covenantal piety — cry to YHWH and receive deliverance. The formula's applicability to all four groups regardless of cause or occupation makes it universal: anyone in extremity who cries to YHWH receives his response.
29: 'He made the storm be still, and the waves of the sea were hushed' — 'made the storm be still' (yaqem searah lidemamah) — the storm that YHWH commanded (v25) is now stilled by his word. The sea's waves 'hushed' (vayehechashu galehem). This direct antecedent of Mark 4:39 ('Peace! Be still!') — Jesus calming the storm uses the same divine prerogative. The disciples' question 'Who then is this?' (Mark 4:41) is answered by recognizing YHWH's storm-command in Ps 107.
30: 'Then they were glad that the waters were quiet, and he brought them to their desired haven' — 'glad that the waters were quiet' (vayishmchu ki yishtaquu) — the joy of relief after terror. 'Their desired haven' (el mehoz chephtzam, to the harbor of their desire) — the safe harbor that was their destination. The ANE image of the harbor as safety and rest: the homeport after the dangerous voyage is the metaphor for covenant rest and the promised land.
31: 'Let them thank the LORD for his steadfast love, for his wondrous works to the children of man!' — the refrain (fourth), now amplified by the full preceding narrative. The wondrous work for the sailors is storm-stilling — the most dramatic of the four rescues because the threat was entirely outside human control. The chesed that governs all four vignettes is now fully displayed.
32: 'Let them extol him in the congregation of the people, and praise him in the assembly of the elders' — the thanksgiving testimony moves to two settings: 'the congregation of the people' (qahal am) — the full assembly — and 'the assembly of the elders' (moshav zeqenim) — the governing council. Both venues are required: the sailors' testimony is not merely private devotion but a public matter affecting the community's understanding of YHWH.
33: 'He turns rivers into a desert, springs of water into thirsty ground' — the psalm's second half (vv33-43) shifts from the four vignettes to creation-scale divine sovereignty. YHWH is the lord of hydrological transformation: the productive made barren. This is the covenant curse dynamic (Deuteronomy 28:23-24: 'the sky over your head shall be bronze, and the earth under you shall be iron') — YHWH's judgment turns fertile land into desert.
34: '...a fruitful land into a salty waste, because of the evil of its inhabitants' — 'fruitful land' (eretz periyah) into 'salty waste' (melechah) — the salting of land as permanent agricultural destruction (Judges 9:45: Abimelech salted Shechem; Deuteronomy 29:23 describes Sodom's salt and sulfur). 'Because of the evil of its inhabitants' (mera'at yoshevim bah) — the explicit covenant-judgment logic: human moral failure produces ecological devastation.
35: 'He turns a desert into pools of water, a parched land into springs of water' — the reverse transformation: barren made fertile. The pair of vv33-34 and vv35-38 shows YHWH's complete sovereignty over both directions: he can curse the productive and bless the barren. Isaiah 41:18 uses identical imagery for the second Exodus: 'I will make the wilderness a pool of water, and the dry land springs of water.'
36: 'And there he lets the hungry dwell, and they establish a city to dwell in' — the 'hungry' (reevim) from v5 return: those who could not find a 'city to dwell in' (v4) are now settled and establish one. The wilderness wanderers of the first vignette find their city; the barren land produces their settlement. The circling back to vignette-one language unifies the psalm.
37: 'They sow fields and plant vineyards and get a fruitful yield' — agricultural restoration: the covenant blessings of Deuteronomy 28:4 ('blessed shall be the fruit of your womb and the fruit of your ground') manifest in the post-exilic return. The planting of vineyards is the symbol of long-term settlement and security (Jeremiah 29:5: 'plant gardens and eat their produce' — Jeremiah's command to the exiles to settle in Babylon precisely because the exile will be long).
38: 'By his blessing they multiply greatly, and he does not let their livestock diminish' — the covenant multiplication blessing (Deuteronomy 28:4: 'blessed shall be the fruit of your womb and the fruit of your livestock'). The community's growth in number is a sign of divine favor and covenant restoration. The livestock's health is a marker of agricultural prosperity in the ancient economy.
39: 'When they are diminished and brought low through oppression, evil, and sorrow' — the turn to adversity: the same community that flourished (vv36-38) can be diminished (yimate'u) through oppression (otzer), evil (ra'ah), and sorrow (yagon). The YHWH who blessed also allows reversal. This verse functions as a hinge: the two possible trajectories for the covenant community.
40: 'He pours contempt on princes and makes them wander in trackless wastes' — YHWH's judgment on the powerful: 'pours contempt on princes' (shophekh buz al-nedivim) — the divine reversal of human status. The nobles (nedivim) who held power are humiliated. 'Wander in trackless wastes' (yata'em bivohu lo-darech) — the wilderness wandering as judgment: the princes who oppressed the humble find themselves in the pathless desert. Job 12:24 uses identical language for YHWH's sovereign overturning of human power.
41: 'But he raises up the needy out of affliction and makes their families like flocks' — the reversal: 'raises up the needy' (yesagev evyon meoni) — the divine exaltation of the humble (1 Samuel 2:8: Hannah's song; Luke 1:52-53: Mary's Magnificat). 'Families like flocks' (mishpachot katzzon) — the restored family's fertility mirroring the growth of sheep. The divine preference for the needy over the powerful is a consistent covenant-theology theme.
42: 'The upright see it and are glad, and all wickedness shuts its mouth' — the two responses to YHWH's reversals: the upright (yesharim) see and rejoice (veyismachu); the wicked (kol-avlah, all wickedness) have nothing to say — their mouths are shut (qapetza piha). The rhetorical reversal: human wisdom (represented by the princes of v40) is silenced by YHWH's judgment; the wisdom of those who fear YHWH is vindicated.
43: 'Whoever is wise, let him attend to these things; let them consider the steadfast love of the LORD' — the wisdom conclusion. The psalm, which began with liturgical thanksgiving, ends with sapiential reflection: the wise person (mi chacham) attends (veyishmor, guards/keeps) to the patterns described and 'considers' (veyitbonenu, from binah, understanding) the chasde YHWH (the steadfast love-acts of YHWH). The four vignettes and the cosmic sovereignty of vv33-42 become material for wisdom meditation. The psalm integrates praise, narrative, and wisdom genres.
